  "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "The report is a true positive. Although SpotBugs flagged a generic utility sink (`ResourceUtils.getFile(URL,String)` at `src/main/java/spark/utils/ResourceUtils.java:219`) rather than the root-cause validation bug, that sink is on a real attacker-controlled path. This is not a B1 false source/sink selection issue: the sink is actually reached on HTTP requests when an application enables `externalStaticFileLocation(...)`. It is also not an A1/A2 reachability artifact: the call chain is concrete and request-driven. The real problem is that Spark's traversal defenses are incomplete: `UriPath.canonical()` only canonicalizes `/`-based segments, while `ExternalResource` later calls `StringUtils.cleanPath()` which converts `\\` to `/` and resolves `..`; then `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal()` uses a naive prefix `startsWith` check, which can be bypassed by sibling-prefix paths. As a result, an attacker can escape the intended static directory and cause arbitrary file read. SpotBugs did not identify the earliest bad line, but the reported sink is still part of the real exploit chain, so the finding should be treated as TP.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Reachable exploit chain: `Service.externalStaticFileLocation()` (`src/main/java/spark/Service.java:358-365`) -> `StaticFilesConfiguration.consumeWithFileResourceHandlers()` (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesConfiguration.java:91-93`) -> `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(String)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:66-83`) -> `ExternalResource(String path)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:39-40`) -> `AbstractFileResolvingResource.isReadable()` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractFileResolvingResource.java:106-113`) -> reported sink `ResourceUtils.getFile(URL,String)` (`src/main/java/spark/utils/ResourceUtils.java:210-219`). The sanitization is insufficient: `UriPath.canonical()` only handles slash-delimited `.`/`..` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/UriPath.java:32-40`), while `StringUtils.cleanPath()` later converts backslashes to slashes and resolves `..` (`src/main/java/spark/utils/StringUtils.java:218-266`). The boundary check is weak: `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal()` only compares normalized strings with `startsWith` (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:18-33`). Example: if the configured external static folder is `/var/www/public`, a request such as `/..%5cpublicity%5csecret.txt` can be turned into `/var/www/publicity/secret.txt`; that path still passes the `startsWith(\"/var/www/public\")` style check even though it is outside the intended directory. Spark then serves the file through `ExternalResource.getInputStream()` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:65-67`). Existing tests only cover simpler traversal attempts like `..\\\\..\\\\spark\\\\` (`src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTestExternal.java:120-125`) and do not cover this sibling-prefix bypass.",
  "new_reason:": ""

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "The report is a true positive. Although the SpotBugs source list is noisy, the sink at `ExternalResource.java:40` is on a real attacker-reachable HTTP static-file path. When external static files are enabled, request-controlled `servletPath/pathInfo` flows through `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(...)` to `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(...)`, then into `new ExternalResource(addedPath)`, and finally into `new File(StringUtils.cleanPath(path))`. The intended sanitization is incomplete: `UriPath.canonical(...)` only treats `/` as a path separator, so `..\\\\...` survives canonicalization, while `StringUtils.cleanPath(...)` later converts `\\\\` to `/` and resolves `..` after the base directory has already been joined. The post-check in `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(...)` is also weak because it uses a lexical `startsWith` check on absolute strings instead of normalized/canonical path containment. An attacker can therefore escape the static root into a sibling directory whose absolute path shares the same prefix as the configured root, causing unauthorized file disclosure.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Relevant code path: `AbstractResourceHandler.java:53-59` reads `request.getServletPath()` / `request.getPathInfo()` and passes the result to `getResource(pathInContext)`; `ExternalResourceHandler.java:66-70` does `path = UriPath.canonical(path); addedPath = addPaths(baseResource, path); resource = new ExternalResource(addedPath);`; `ExternalResource.java:39-40` does `file = new File(StringUtils.cleanPath(path));`; `StaticFilesConfiguration.java:91-103` opens `resource.getInputStream()` and copies it to the HTTP response. The guard is flawed: `UriPath.java:32-160` canonicalizes only `/`-delimited segments, while `StringUtils.java:218-255` later converts backslashes to `/` and collapses `..`. Then `DirectoryTraversal.java:18-33` only checks whether `absolutePath.startsWith(absoluteFolder)`. Concrete exploit: if the app configures external static root `/var/www/public` and a readable file exists at `/var/www/public_backup/secret.txt`, a request such as `/..%5Cpublic_backup%5Csecret.txt` can be decoded to `/..\\\\public_backup\\\\secret.txt`; `UriPath.canonical` leaves it unchanged, `cleanPath` turns `/var/www/public/..\\\\public_backup\\\\secret.txt` into `/var/www/public_backup/secret.txt`, and the `startsWith` check wrongly accepts it because `/var/www/public_backup/secret.txt` starts with `/var/www/public`. The project's own test at `StaticFilesTestExternal.java:120-125` does not disprove this: it tries `..\\\\..\\\\spark\\\\Spark.class`, which is a different target from the created outside file at `StaticFilesTestExternal.java:86-91`, so that test misses the prefix-sharing sibling case.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report is a false positive for Spark as shipped. `ResourceUtils.getFile(URL, String)` is a generic URL-to-`File` converter, and SpotBugs marks `URL.getFile()` / the `resourceUrl` parameter as tainted, but in this project those values are not attacker-controlled. The only production callers are `AbstractFileResolvingResource.getFile()` and `getFileForLastModifiedCheck()`, and the URLs they pass come from internally created resource objects (`ExternalResource.getURL()` uses `file.toURI().toURL()`, and `ClassPathResource.getURL()` uses classloader-resolved resources). In the real HTTP static-file flow, request paths are canonicalized with `UriPath.canonical(...)` and then checked with `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainst...(...)` before a resource is returned, so traversal attempts are stopped before this utility is involved. Therefore the flagged sink does not correspond to an exploitable CWE-022 path in the project.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1"
  ],
  "evidence": "Flagged code: `ResourceUtils.getFile(URL, String)` rejects non-`file` URLs at `src/main/java/spark/utils/ResourceUtils.java:212-217`, then uses `new File(...)`; the reported line `222` is only a fallback after `toURI(resourceUrl)` throws `URISyntaxException` (`:218-223`). Real callers are only `src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractFileResolvingResource.java:44-46` and `:54-58`. URL producers are internal: `src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:76-78` returns `file.toURI().toURL()`, and `src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResource.java:183-193` returns a classloader resource URL. Request-controlled paths are normalized and bounded earlier: `src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:66-83` and `src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResourceHandler.java:67-84` call `UriPath.canonical(path)` and then `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(...)` / `protectAgainstInClassPath(...)`. `src/main/java/spark/resource/UriPath.java` returns `null` when `..` would escape above root, and tests confirm this in `src/test/java/spark/resource/UriPathTest.java` (for example `/../foo -> null`, `/foo/../../bar -> null`). `src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTestExternal.java` also verifies a traversal attempt returns 404 instead of escaping the configured static root. Additionally, there are no production call sites that pass user input into `ResourceUtils.getFile(String)`; repo-wide search shows only the internal resource flow and tests.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

{
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "This is a false positive. At the reported sink, `ResourceUtils.getFile(URI, String)` is only a generic URI-to-`File` conversion helper: it checks that the URI scheme is `file` and then builds a `File` from the scheme-specific part. That alone is not an exploitable CWE-022 in Spark, because the method does not enforce or violate any security boundary by itself and `new File(...)` does not open or read the file. More importantly, repo-level reachability analysis shows the flagged `URI` overload is not used by Spark's production request-handling paths. Production resource resolution uses the `URL` overload instead, and the real user-controlled static-file path goes through canonicalization and explicit directory-traversal protection before any file is served. So the report depends on shallow interprocedural taint reasoning rather than a real exploitable flow in this project.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "A1"
  ],
  "evidence": "Flagged code: `src/main/java/spark/utils/ResourceUtils.java:250-258` -> `if (!URL_PROTOCOL_FILE.equals(resourceUri.getScheme())) throw ...; return new File(resourceUri.getSchemeSpecificPart());`. This method is only called by the wrapper at `ResourceUtils.java:235-236`; a repo-wide inspection found no production caller of `ResourceUtils.getFile(URI, ...)`. The production path for resource resolution is `src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractFileResolvingResource.java:44-46`, which calls `ResourceUtils.getFile(url, getDescription())` (the `URL` overload, not the flagged `URI` overload). The actual request-driven static-file path is `src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java` -> `src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:65-83`, where Spark canonicalizes the request path with `UriPath.canonical(path)` and then enforces `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(resource.getPath(), baseResource)`. That guard is implemented at `src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:18-23`. Also, the external base directory is configured by application code at startup via `Service.externalStaticFileLocation(...)` (`src/main/java/spark/Service.java:358-365`) and `StaticFilesConfiguration.configureExternal(...)` (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesConfiguration.java:160-175`), not from attacker-controlled HTTP input.",
  "new_reason:": ""
